title: Why Entangled Spins Disagree on Every Axis

metadata.color_law: TEAL = the singlet state (rotationally invariant, perfect anti-correlation); CRIMSON = the triplet (not rotationally invariant, does not give perfect anti-correlation on every axis)
metadata.note: Exclusions: no ladder construction of the singlet, no CHSH/Bell inequality derivation, no triplet comparison algebra
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Teardown register (voices/teardown/VOICE.md + CLAUDE-BRAND.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)
swap the outro to the Claude outro from AUTHOR.MD :: NikBearBrown
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

skin_warnings: B01: palette=claude but the cold open is 'un-annotated' — COLD OPEN LAW wants ClaudeComposerAsk
B12: palette=claude but the outro is 'OutroCTA' — OUTRO LAW wants ClaudeTitleOutro

beats.narration_text: This is Liam, in for Bear. Two particles in a singlet state have no spin of their own — yet measure them along any direction you pick, and they always come out opposite. Every axis. Every time.

card.title: Why Entangled Spins Disagree on Every Axis

build.suggested: suggested prompt: This is Liam, in for Bear. Two particles in a singlet state have no spin of their own — yet measure them along any direction you pick, and they always come out

beats.act: COLD OPEN — the singlet state has total spin zero
beats.narration_text: The singlet state is a superposition: up-down minus down-up, divided by root two. It is the one two-particle spin state with total angular momentum exactly zero. No preferred direction. No axis.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: The singlet state is a superposition: up-down minus down-up, divided by root two. It is the one two-particle spin state with total angular momentum exactly zero

beats.narration_text: Measure particle one along z. You get up half the time. But then particle two is always down. Measure along x instead. You get up half the time — but particle two is always left. Opposite, always.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Measure particle one along z. You get up half the time. But then particle two is always down. Measure along x instead. You get up half the time — but particle t

beats.narration_text: The singlet has no preferred axis. Measure along z, x, y, or any diagonal — the two spins always disagree. Why does anti-correlation hold on every axis, not just the one you prepared the state along?

card.title: Why does the singlet give perfect anti-correlation on every measurement axis?

build.suggested: suggested prompt: The singlet has no preferred axis. Measure along z, x, y, or any diagonal — the two spins always disagree. Why does anti-correlation hold on every axis, not jus

beats.act: THE PROBLEM — why just z is not enough
beats.narration_text: You might expect anti-correlation only on the z-axis, since the state is built from up-z and down-z. But the triplet state — up-down plus down-up — is also built from the same pieces and does not give anti-correlation on the x-axis. Something is different about the singlet.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: You might expect anti-correlation only on the z-axis, since the state is built from up-z and down-z. But the triplet state — up-down plus down-up — is also buil

beats.act: THE MECHANISM — the singlet written in the x-basis
beats.narration_text: Write the singlet in the x-basis. The x-eigenstates are right and left — superpositions of up and down. Substitute them into the singlet. The cross terms cancel exactly. You get right-left minus left-right, divided by root two: the same form, different axis.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Write the singlet in the x-basis. The x-eigenstates are right and left — superpositions of up and down. Substitute them into the singlet. The cross terms cancel

beats.narration_text: This is not coincidence. The singlet has total angular momentum zero, which means it is rotationally invariant — it looks the same in every basis. Whatever axis you choose to measure, the singlet is still the same form: particle-one-plus, particle-two-minus.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: This is not coincidence. The singlet has total angular momentum zero, which means it is rotationally invariant — it looks the same in every basis. Whatever axis

beats.narration_text: This is the heart of the EPR argument. The particles are correlated on every axis simultaneously. But no hidden instruction book could tell each particle what to say for every possible axis — the Bell inequalities rule that out. The correlations are genuinely quantum.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: This is the heart of the EPR argument. The particles are correlated on every axis simultaneously. But no hidden instruction book could tell each particle what t

beats.narration_text: A student measures two singlet particles along three axes: z, x, and a diagonal at 45 degrees. Each time, the two results disagree. She rewrites the singlet in each basis and finds it has the same anti-correlated form every time. The axis does not matter. The state is the same state regardless of which way you point your detector.

beats.narration_text: The singlet is the unique two-spin state with total angular momentum zero. A state with zero total angular momentum looks the same in every basis. That rotational invariance is why the perfect anti-correlation holds on every axis — not just the one you prepared.

card.title: Total angular momentum zero means the same form on every axis.
